cards.question: What certificates and documents must you have in your personal possession to act as PIC?
cards.answer: You must carry your pilot certificate (or temporary certificate), a valid photo ID, and a current medical certificate (or BasicMed compliance letter). The mnemonic is 'photo ID, pilot certificate, medical.' Per 14 CFR 61.3, failure to present these on request by the FAA, NTSB, or law enforcement is a violation. Your logbook does not need to be carried but should be available to document currency.

cards.question: What are the passenger-carrying currency requirements for a private pilot?
cards.answer: To carry passengers, you must have made at least three takeoffs and three landings within the preceding 90 days in an aircraft of the same category, class, and type (if required). For night passengers, those landings must be to a full stop and performed during the period from one hour after sunset to one hour before sunrise. This ensures recent experience in the specific flight regime you'll be operating in.

cards.question: Your third-class medical was issued on your 38th birthday. When does it expire for private pilot privileges?
cards.answer: For operations requiring a third-class medical where you are under 40, the medical is valid for 60 calendar months. Since it was issued on your 38th birthday, it expires at the end of the 60th calendar month after issuance—effectively on the last day of the month, five years later. If you were 40 or older at the time of examination, it would only be valid for 24 calendar months.

cards.question: What is BasicMed and who qualifies?
cards.answer: BasicMed allows pilots to fly without holding a traditional FAA medical certificate, provided they held a valid medical at any point after July 14, 2006, and it was not revoked or suspended. You must complete a medical exam with a state-licensed physician every 48 months and an online aeromedical course every 24 months. BasicMed limits you to aircraft with no more than 6 seats, 6,000 lbs max takeoff weight, flights below 18,000 feet MSL and 250 knots, and carrying no more than 5 passengers.

cards.question: When is a flight review required and what does it consist of?
cards.answer: A flight review is required every 24 calendar months per 14 CFR 61.56. It must include at least one hour of flight training and one hour of ground training that reviews the current general operating and flight rules of Part 91. There is no checkride or pass/fail—the instructor endorses your logbook if you demonstrate satisfactory proficiency. Completing a pilot proficiency check, new certificate, or Wings phase also satisfies the flight review requirement.

cards.question: Can a private pilot receive compensation for flying?
cards.answer: Generally, no. A private pilot may not act as PIC for compensation or hire. However, there are specific exceptions: you may share operating expenses (fuel, oil, airport fees, rental fees) equally with passengers who have a common purpose for the trip. You may also fly in connection with a business if flying is only incidental to that business. Additionally, you can act as PIC for charitable, community, or aerial work events under specific conditions in 14 CFR 91.146 and 91.147.

cards.question: You want to fly a high-performance airplane. What do you need?
cards.answer: To act as PIC of a high-performance airplane (one with an engine of more than 200 horsepower), you must have received and logged ground and flight training from an authorized instructor and received a one-time logbook endorsement per 14 CFR 61.31(f). This endorsement does not expire, but you must maintain currency in the aircraft category and class. High-performance training typically covers engine management, manifold pressure, and the additional considerations of higher-powered operations.

cards.question: What endorsement is needed to fly a complex airplane?
cards.answer: You need a one-time logbook endorsement from an authorized instructor certifying you have received ground and flight training in a complex airplane and are proficient. A complex airplane has retractable landing gear, flaps, and a controllable-pitch propeller. This endorsement is separate from the high-performance endorsement—you may need both if the aircraft qualifies as both complex and high-performance.

cards.question: Your pilot certificate has been lost. Can you still fly?
cards.answer: Yes, but you must request a replacement from the FAA. In the meantime, you may obtain a temporary certificate by calling the Airmen Certification Branch or using the FAA's online portal. You can also exercise the privileges of your certificate for up to 60 days while the replacement is in transit, provided you have a record of your application. You still must carry your photo ID and medical certificate.

cards.question: A private pilot is convicted of a DUI. What are the notification requirements?
cards.answer: Under 14 CFR 61.15, you must provide a written report of any alcohol or drug-related motor vehicle action (conviction or administrative action) to the FAA Civil Aviation Security Division within 60 calendar days. Failure to report is grounds for suspension or revocation of all certificates. Two or more alcohol-related offenses within a three-year period are also grounds for certificate action. This applies regardless of whether the offense affected your flying.

cards.question: What are the privileges and limitations of a private pilot certificate?
cards.answer: A private pilot may act as PIC of an aircraft for which they are rated, carry passengers, and fly at night (with proper currency). Limitations include: you cannot fly for compensation or hire (with narrow exceptions), you cannot act as a required crewmember for an airline, and you are limited to aircraft in the category and class on your certificate. You must also hold at least a current third-class medical or comply with BasicMed.

cards.question: Do you need a logbook endorsement to fly a tailwheel airplane?
cards.answer: Yes. Per 14 CFR 61.31(i), you must receive ground and flight training from an authorized instructor in a tailwheel airplane and receive a logbook endorsement before acting as PIC. This endorsement is one-time and does not expire. The training covers the unique ground-handling characteristics, takeoff techniques, and landing procedures specific to tailwheel aircraft.

cards.question: What would disqualify you from holding a medical certificate?
cards.answer: Certain conditions are specifically disqualifying under 14 CFR 67, including epilepsy, psychosis, substance dependence, and certain cardiac conditions. However, many conditions can receive a Special Issuance authorization if you can demonstrate the condition is adequately controlled. You must report all visits to healthcare professionals and all medications on your MedXPress application. Falsifying a medical application is a federal offense and grounds for revocation.

cards.question: Can you fly as PIC if you are taking an over-the-counter allergy medication?
cards.answer: It depends on the medication. Many antihistamines (like diphenhydramine/Benadryl) are disqualifying because they cause drowsiness and impair cognitive function. Newer non-sedating antihistamines like loratadine (Claritin) are generally acceptable after a suitable waiting period. The underlying condition also matters—if you are too symptomatic to fly safely, the medication question is moot. When in doubt, consult an AME or call the AOPA medical services line.

cards.question: You plan to fly to the Bahamas. What additional requirements apply to an international flight?
cards.answer: For international flights, you should file an international flight plan, carry your passport, and review customs and immigration requirements. You must land at an Airport of Entry (AOE) and file an eAPIS (electronic Advance Passenger Information System) manifest with CBP before departing and returning. You should also be familiar with ICAO flight plan formats, the destination country's aviation regulations, and carry required survival equipment for overwater flights.

cards.question: How long after scuba diving should you wait before flying?
cards.answer: The AIM recommends waiting at least 12 hours after a non-decompression dive and 24 hours after a decompression dive before flying above 8,000 feet cabin altitude. This is because reduced pressure at altitude can cause dissolved nitrogen to form bubbles in the body (decompression sickness). If you flew in a pressurized aircraft, the cabin altitude still matters—most are pressurized to around 6,000-8,000 feet.

cards.question: As a newly certificated private pilot, can you immediately fly at night?
cards.answer: Yes, the private pilot certificate includes night privileges. Your training should have included at least 3 hours of night flight training including 10 takeoffs and landings to a full stop, and a night cross-country of over 100 NM. To carry passengers at night, you must be night-current: three full-stop takeoffs and landings in the preceding 90 days during the period from one hour after sunset to one hour before sunrise in the same category and class.

cards.question: What are the required endorsements for a student pilot to solo?
cards.answer: Before solo, a student pilot needs: (1) a pre-solo knowledge test endorsement, (2) a pre-solo flight training endorsement, and (3) the instructor's endorsement in the student's logbook for that specific make and model. For solo cross-country, additional endorsements are required for the specific route and for cross-country flight in general. These endorsements are valid for 90 days and must be renewed by the instructor.

cards.question: When must you log flight time?
cards.answer: You must log flight time that is used to meet the requirements for a certificate, rating, or flight review, or to meet recent experience (currency) requirements. Per 14 CFR 61.51, this includes solo time, PIC time, second-in-command time, flight training, instrument time, and night time. You are not required to log every flight, but it is strongly recommended for insurance and record-keeping purposes. Each entry must include date, total flight time, location, type of experience, and aircraft identification.

cards.question: Can you carry passengers if your flight review expired yesterday?
cards.answer: No. You cannot act as PIC at all, with or without passengers, if your flight review has lapsed. Per 14 CFR 61.56, no person may act as PIC unless they have completed a flight review within the preceding 24 calendar months. You may still fly as a passenger or act as SIC if qualified. To regain PIC privileges, you must complete a flight review with an instructor.

cards.question: What documents must be on board the aircraft before flight?
cards.answer: The required documents can be remembered with the acronym ARROW: Airworthiness certificate (displayed), Registration (current, and signed), Radio station license (for international flights), Operating limitations (POH/AFM, placards, or markings), and Weight and balance data (current). The airworthiness certificate never expires as long as the aircraft is maintained in an airworthy condition per its type certificate and applicable regulations.

cards.question: How do you determine if an aircraft is airworthy for flight?
cards.answer: An aircraft is airworthy when it conforms to its type certificate and is in a condition for safe operation. You verify this by confirming all required inspections are current (annual, 100-hour if applicable, AD compliance), the aircraft has no known defects, all required equipment is operational or properly deferred, and the weight and balance is within limits for the planned flight. The pilot is the final authority—even if maintenance records are current, if something seems unsafe during preflight, the aircraft should not fly.

cards.question: What inspections are required to keep an aircraft airworthy, and how often?
cards.answer: The required inspections can be remembered with the acronym AV1ATE: Annual inspection (every 12 calendar months), VOR check (every 30 days, IFR only), 100-hour inspection (if used for hire or flight instruction), Altimeter/static system (every 24 calendar months, IFR only), Transponder check (every 24 calendar months), and ELT inspection (every 12 calendar months, with battery replacement per 91.207). For a VFR private pilot, the annual, transponder, and ELT checks are the critical ones.

cards.question: What is the difference between an annual inspection and a 100-hour inspection?
cards.answer: Both are comprehensive inspections covering the same scope of work per 14 CFR 43 Appendix D. The key differences are: (1) an annual must be performed by an IA (Inspection Authorization) holder, while a 100-hour can be done by any A&P mechanic; (2) a 100-hour is only required for aircraft used for hire or flight instruction for hire; (3) the 100-hour has a 10-hour grace period for ferrying to a maintenance facility, but those hours count against the next 100; and (4) a 100-hour cannot substitute for an annual, but an annual resets the 100-hour clock.

cards.question: You discover during preflight that the landing light is burned out. Can you still fly?
cards.answer: It depends on whether the landing light is required equipment. Use the decision flow of 14 CFR 91.213(d) for aircraft without an MEL: check if it's required by the type certificate, 14 CFR 91.205, an AD, or any other regulation. Landing lights are required for aircraft operated for hire at night (91.205), but not for private VFR day operations. If not required, you can placard it inoperative, disable it or remove it, and note it in the maintenance records. A qualified mechanic or the pilot (if considered preventive maintenance) may do this.

cards.question: Walk me through the 91.213(d) decision process for inoperative equipment when there is no MEL.
cards.answer: First, determine if the item is required by the VFR-day or VFR-night equipment list in 91.205. Second, check if it's required by the aircraft's type certificate or kind of operation. Third, check for applicable Airworthiness Directives. Fourth, check if it's required by any other Part 91 regulation (like a transponder in certain airspace). If the item is not required by any of these, you may placard it inoperative, and if the item is a required instrument, it must be deactivated or removed by an appropriately rated person. A maintenance record entry must be made.

cards.question: What equipment is required for VFR day flight?
cards.answer: 14 CFR 91.205(b) lists required instruments and equipment, remembered by the mnemonic ATOMATOFLAMES: Airspeed indicator, Tachometer, Oil pressure gauge, Manifold pressure gauge (if applicable), Altimeter, Temperature gauge (for liquid-cooled engines), Oil temperature gauge, Fuel gauge for each tank, Landing gear position indicator (if retractable), Anti-collision light system (for aircraft certificated after 3/11/96), Magnetic compass, ELT, and Seatbelts (including shoulder harness for front seats manufactured after 1978).

cards.question: What additional equipment is required for VFR night flight beyond day VFR?
cards.answer: Per 14 CFR 91.205(c), night VFR adds: Fuses (one spare set or three of each kind), Landing light (if operated for hire), Anti-collision lights, Position lights (nav lights), and a Source of electrical energy (adequate for all installed electrical and radio equipment). The mnemonic is FLAPS. Note that a landing light is only required for hire operations at night, but having one is strongly recommended for safety.

cards.question: What is an Airworthiness Directive, and how do you check compliance?
cards.answer: An AD is a legally enforceable FAA regulation that requires inspection, modification, or a specific action on an aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance to correct an unsafe condition. ADs can be one-time, recurring, or emergency. Compliance is documented in the aircraft maintenance records. Before flight, you should review the maintenance records to verify all applicable ADs have been complied with. You can search current ADs on the FAA's AD database by aircraft make and model.

cards.question: What preventive maintenance can a private pilot perform on an aircraft they own?
cards.answer: Per 14 CFR 43 Appendix A, a certificated pilot (who is also the owner or operator) may perform limited preventive maintenance including: replenishing hydraulic fluid, servicing landing gear wheel bearings, servicing shock struts, replacing bulbs and lenses for position and landing lights, replacing safety wire, lubricating items not requiring disassembly, making simple fabric patches, replacing prefabricated fuel lines, cleaning or replacing spark plugs, and replacing batteries. The pilot must make a logbook entry per 14 CFR 43.12.

cards.question: What is an MEL and how does it differ from a KOEL?
cards.answer: A Minimum Equipment List (MEL) is an FAA-approved document specific to an operator that lists equipment permitted to be inoperative under certain conditions. It's derived from the Master MEL and is more restrictive. Most GA aircraft don't have MELs. A Kinds of Operations Equipment List (KOEL) is found in the POH and lists required equipment for specific operations (VFR day, VFR night, IFR, etc.). If your aircraft has an MEL, use it. If not, follow the 91.213(d) process.

cards.question: What is a Special Flight Permit, and when might you need one?
cards.answer: A Special Flight Permit (ferry permit) allows an aircraft that is not currently airworthy to be flown to a specific location for maintenance, storage, or delivery. It might be needed if an aircraft's annual inspection has lapsed or if it has a known condition that makes it technically unairworthy but still safe for a one-time flight. You apply through your local FSDO, and the permit specifies the route, conditions, and limitations for the flight.

cards.question: Where do you find the operating limitations for your aircraft?
cards.answer: Operating limitations are found in the POH/AFM (Pilot's Operating Handbook/Airplane Flight Manual) approved by the FAA, as well as on placards and instrument markings within the aircraft. These are part of the type certificate and must be followed. They include speed limitations (Vne, Va, etc.), weight limits, CG range, approved maneuvers, fuel and oil requirements, and operational restrictions. The POH/AFM must be on board for every flight per 14 CFR 91.9.

cards.question: What is the significance of the aircraft registration, and when must it be renewed?
cards.answer: Aircraft registration establishes U.S. nationality and ownership. It must be carried on board and is valid for 7 years from the date of issue under the current system (since 2010). Previously, it did not expire. The aircraft must display its N-number in compliance with 14 CFR 45. An aircraft cannot legally be flown with an expired registration. You renew through the FAA Aircraft Registration Branch, and you'll receive renewal reminders before expiration.

cards.question: You're renting an aircraft and notice the transponder inspection is 25 months old. Can you fly?
cards.answer: No, you cannot fly in any airspace that requires a transponder. Per 14 CFR 91.413, the transponder must have been tested and inspected within the preceding 24 calendar months. Even if you're flying in airspace where a transponder is not required, the FAA expects all installed transponders to be inspected on schedule. You should notify the rental facility that the transponder check has lapsed before dispatching the aircraft.

cards.question: What is a progressive inspection program?
cards.answer: A progressive inspection is an FAA-approved alternative to the standard annual/100-hour inspection. It divides the total annual inspection workload into smaller segments performed at more frequent intervals, so the aircraft is never out of service for an extended period. It's commonly used by flight schools and commercial operators. The program must be approved by the local FSDO and the entire aircraft must be inspected within each 12-month period.

cards.question: Can an aircraft with an expired ELT battery be flown?
cards.answer: Not legally for most flights. Per 14 CFR 91.207, the ELT must be inspected within 12 months and the battery must be replaced (or recharged) when the transmitter has been used for more than 1 cumulative hour, or when 50% of the useful life has expired. However, there are exceptions to the ELT requirement itself: flights within 50 NM of the home airport, training flights, new aircraft ferrying, and certain other situations listed in 91.207(e). If your ELT battery is expired, you could still fly under those exceptions.

cards.question: What is the difference between a type certificate and a supplemental type certificate?
cards.answer: A Type Certificate (TC) is issued by the FAA when a new aircraft design meets all applicable airworthiness standards. It defines the aircraft's design, limitations, and approved configuration. A Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) approves a modification to an existing type-certificated aircraft—such as installing a new engine, avionics upgrade, or adding tip tanks. The STC holder is responsible for the modification's airworthiness, and the installation must be documented in the aircraft records.

cards.question: What placards are required in a typical training aircraft?
cards.answer: Required placards vary by aircraft but typically include: fuel type and capacity per tank, no-smoking placards, baggage weight limitations, maneuvering speed (Va), spin approval or prohibition, passenger briefing card location, oxygen requirements (if applicable), and any limitations from installed STCs. Placards are part of the operating limitations and are required by the type certificate. Missing or illegible placards can make an aircraft unairworthy.

cards.question: A mechanic just completed the annual inspection on your aircraft. What entry should you look for in the maintenance records?
cards.answer: The maintenance record entry must include the type of inspection (annual), the date, aircraft total time in service, the signature and certificate number of the IA who performed the inspection, and a statement that the aircraft has been inspected in accordance with an annual inspection and was found to be in airworthy condition (or a list of discrepancies for items not airworthy). The IA's approval for return to service is what makes the aircraft legal to fly. You should also verify that all discrepancies found were corrected.

cards.question: Walk me through how you would obtain a weather briefing for today's flight.
cards.answer: I would start by checking the big picture using the Aviation Weather Center (aviationweather.gov) for the area forecast discussion and surface analysis. Then I would obtain a Standard Briefing through 1800wxbrief.com or the Leidos Flight Service app, which provides: adverse conditions, synopsis, current conditions (METARs), forecast conditions (TAFs), winds aloft, NOTAMs, and ATC delays. I would evaluate the route segment by segment, check for TFRs, and make a go/no-go decision based on my personal minimums. I would also check PIREPs along the route for real-world pilot observations.

cards.question: What are the three types of weather briefings you can request from Flight Service?
cards.answer: Standard Briefing is the most complete and should be requested when you haven't received previous information or need a full update. It includes adverse conditions, VFR flight not recommended (if applicable), synopsis, current conditions, forecast, winds aloft, NOTAMs, and ATC delays. Abbreviated Briefing is requested when you already have information and need to supplement or update it. Outlook Briefing is requested for flights planned 6+ hours in advance, providing a general overview of conditions expected during the proposed flight.

cards.question: You receive this METAR: KXYZ 151753Z 18012G20KT 3SM BR BKN008 OVC015 18/17 A2990. Describe the conditions.
cards.answer: This METAR was observed at KXYZ on the 15th at 1753 Zulu. Winds are from 180° at 12 knots gusting to 20. Visibility is 3 statute miles in mist. There is a broken ceiling at 800 feet AGL and overcast layer at 1,500 feet AGL. Temperature is 18°C, dewpoint 17°C (very close, indicating high moisture and potential fog development). Altimeter setting is 29.90 inHg. These are marginal VFR conditions and the 1°C temperature-dewpoint spread is a significant concern for further deterioration.

cards.question: What is the difference between an AIRMET, a SIGMET, and a Convective SIGMET?
cards.answer: AIRMETs (Airman's Meteorological Information) warn of weather significant to light aircraft: moderate icing (Zulu), moderate turbulence and sustained surface winds >30 knots (Tango), and IFR conditions/mountain obscuration (Sierra). They are valid for 6 hours. SIGMETs warn of non-convective weather significant to ALL aircraft: severe icing, severe or extreme turbulence, volcanic ash, and dust storms reducing visibility below 3 SM. They are valid for 4 hours. Convective SIGMETs cover thunderstorm-related hazards: tornadoes, lines of thunderstorms, embedded thunderstorms, and severe thunderstorms with hail >3/4 inch or wind gusts >50 knots. They are valid for 2 hours.

cards.question: How do you read a TAF, and how does it differ from a METAR?
cards.answer: A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is a forecast of conditions at an airport for a 24 or 30-hour period, while a METAR is an actual observation of current conditions. TAFs use similar coding but include change groups: FM (from—permanent change), TEMPO (temporary fluctuation, usually less than an hour), BECMG (becoming—gradual change over a period), and PROB (probability percentage). TAFs include expected wind, visibility, weather, and cloud conditions. A TAF covers a 5 SM radius of the airport center.

cards.question: Why are PIREPs so valuable, and when should you file one?
cards.answer: PIREPs (Pilot Reports) are invaluable because they provide real-time observations from pilots actually flying through the conditions—no forecast or model can substitute for actual experience. They're especially critical for confirming or denying icing, turbulence, cloud tops/bases, and visibility aloft. You should file a PIREP any time you encounter conditions that differ from the forecast, any time you encounter icing or turbulence, and any time you can report cloud tops or bases. PIREPs are also the primary source of icing and turbulence information between reporting stations. File through ATC or Flight Service on 122.0.

cards.question: You're planning a flight and the forecast calls for scattered thunderstorms along your route. How do you evaluate this?
cards.answer: Scattered thunderstorms cover 25-54% of the area, meaning there will be gaps to navigate around—but this is still serious weather. I would check radar imagery, convective outlooks, and the timing of convective development. I would look for areas of higher instability (lifted index, CAPE values) to identify the most likely storm development areas. I would plan an alternate route if possible, establish personal minimums (I won't try to thread between cells), ensure I have onboard weather (ADS-B In or XM) for in-flight updates, and be prepared to divert or turn back. A 'scattered' forecast can quickly become 'widespread' if conditions change.

cards.question: What weather conditions produce carburetor icing, and how do you detect and handle it?
cards.answer: Carburetor icing can occur at outside air temperatures between roughly 20°F and 70°F when humidity is high. It forms when the pressure and temperature drop in the venturi of the carburetor cause moisture to freeze, blocking airflow. You detect it by a gradual decrease in RPM (fixed-pitch prop) or manifold pressure (constant-speed prop). To address it, apply full carburetor heat—expect a further temporary RPM drop as the ice melts, followed by a rise back to normal. Use carb heat preventively when operating in conditions conducive to icing, especially at reduced power settings.

cards.question: What types of fog might you encounter, and how does each form?
cards.answer: Radiation fog forms on clear, calm nights when the ground radiates heat and cools the air above it to its dewpoint—it typically burns off after sunrise. Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves over a cooler surface (common along coastlines). Upslope fog forms when moist air is forced up sloping terrain and cools adiabatically. Precipitation-induced fog (frontal fog) forms when warm rain falls through cooler air near the surface. Steam fog forms when cold air moves over warm water. Each type has different dissipation characteristics; radiation fog tends to lift by mid-morning, while advection fog can persist for days.

cards.question: The temperature-dewpoint spread is 3°C and decreasing. What does this tell you?
cards.answer: A narrowing temperature-dewpoint spread indicates increasing relative humidity, and when the spread reaches 0°C, the air is saturated and fog, low clouds, or precipitation become likely. The general rule is that the temperature-dewpoint spread decreases about 2.5°C per 1,000 feet of altitude gain (or as evening cooling occurs). With only a 3°C spread and it decreasing, you should expect visibility restrictions soon—potentially fog forming within the next hour or two, especially near sunset. This would heavily influence my go/no-go decision.

cards.question: Describe the weather you would expect with an approaching warm front.
cards.answer: As a warm front approaches, you typically see a predictable sequence: first high cirrus clouds, then cirrostratus (possibly with a halo), then altostratus, and finally nimbostratus with steady rain or drizzle. Visibility gradually decreases, ceilings lower, and conditions can deteriorate to IFR well ahead of the surface front—sometimes 200+ miles. Warm fronts move slowly and the poor weather can persist for an extended period. Icing can be a hazard in the cloud layers. After passage, expect warmer temperatures, southerly winds, and improving conditions, though haze may persist.

cards.question: How does a cold front differ from a warm front in terms of weather hazards?
cards.answer: Cold fronts typically move faster (15-30 knots vs 10-15 for warm fronts) and produce more violent but shorter-duration weather. Expect a narrow band of cumuliform clouds (possibly cumulonimbus with thunderstorms), gusty winds, wind shear, turbulence, heavy rain, and rapid weather changes. The frontal zone is narrower (50-100 miles) compared to a warm front's broad area. After passage, conditions improve rapidly with cooler temperatures, northwest winds, and clearing skies. Fast-moving cold fronts and squall lines are among the most dangerous weather for GA pilots.

cards.question: What is the difference between a stable and unstable atmosphere, and how does each affect flying?
cards.answer: A stable atmosphere resists vertical motion—air displaced vertically returns to its original position. It produces stratiform clouds, steady precipitation, reduced visibility in haze/fog, and smooth air. An unstable atmosphere encourages vertical motion—displaced air continues to rise. It produces cumuliform clouds, showery precipitation, good visibility outside of precipitation, and turbulence. Temperature determines stability: if the environmental lapse rate exceeds the adiabatic lapse rate, the atmosphere is unstable. Pilots should expect bumpy conditions in unstable air and low visibility in stable air.

cards.question: What is density altitude and why should every pilot understand it?
cards.answer: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperature. It represents the altitude at which the aircraft 'thinks' it is flying in terms of air density. High density altitude (caused by high temperature, high elevation, high humidity, or low pressure) degrades all aspects of aircraft performance: longer takeoff rolls, reduced climb rates, higher true airspeeds for the same indicated airspeed, and longer landing distances. On a hot summer day at a high-elevation airport, a normally capable aircraft might not be able to outclimb terrain. Always calculate density altitude before flight.

cards.question: You encounter unexpected IMC conditions during a VFR flight. What should you do?
cards.answer: First, maintain aircraft control—trust your instruments and resist the temptation to look outside. Fly the aircraft. Execute a 180° standard-rate turn to reverse course back to VFR conditions. If that isn't possible, maintain wings level, climb to a safe altitude, and contact ATC immediately on the current frequency or 121.5 for assistance. Declare an emergency—you are now in a potentially fatal situation and ATC will provide all available assistance. Don't let pride prevent you from using 'Mayday.' The number one cause of fatal GA accidents is VFR into IMC, and the average survival time for a non-instrument-rated pilot in IMC is just 178 seconds.

cards.question: What is a microburst and why is it so dangerous?
cards.answer: A microburst is a small-scale, intense downdraft that spreads outward in all directions upon reaching the surface, producing severe wind shear. It can last 5-15 minutes and produce headwind/tailwind changes of 30-45 knots within a very small area. On approach, you first experience a headwind (increased performance), followed by a downdraft and then a tailwind (rapidly decreasing performance)—this sequence can push you below the glidepath and into the ground. Microbursts are associated with both convective weather and virga (rain evaporating before reaching the surface). Avoidance is the only safe strategy.

cards.question: How do you interpret the winds and temperatures aloft forecast?
cards.answer: Winds aloft forecasts (FB) give wind direction, speed, and temperature at selected altitudes. Direction is in tens of degrees referenced to true north, speed in knots. When direction is coded 9900, winds are light and variable. Temperatures are in Celsius and are not forecast for altitudes within 2,500 feet of the station elevation. Temperatures above 24,000 feet are always negative so the minus sign is omitted. If the wind group starts with a number from 51-86, subtract 50 from the direction and add 100 to the speed—this indicates winds over 100 knots. Use these forecasts for flight planning: groundspeed calculations and choosing optimal altitudes.

cards.question: What are your personal weather minimums, and how did you arrive at them?
cards.answer: Personal minimums should be higher than the regulatory VFR minimums and based on your experience level, comfort, and proficiency. For example, a new private pilot might set personal minimums of 3,000 foot ceilings, 5 SM visibility, crosswinds under 10 knots, and no convective activity within 50 miles of the route. As you gain experience and additional training, these can be gradually adjusted. The PAVE checklist (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures) is a useful framework for this evaluation. The key is to establish these minimums in advance, not in the heat of a go/no-go decision.

cards.question: You see virga near the airport. What concerns does this raise?
cards.answer: Virga—precipitation evaporating before reaching the surface—indicates several hazards. The evaporation process cools the air rapidly, potentially creating microbursts and strong downdrafts. Wind shear near the surface can be severe and unpredictable. Additionally, the dry air below the clouds suggests low-level turbulence. If virga is observed near your intended airport, be prepared for sudden wind shear on approach, consider delaying your arrival, and listen for PIREPS or LLWAS (Low-Level Wind Shear Alert System) reports from the tower. Avoid flying directly beneath or through virga.

cards.question: What is wind shear and how might you recognize it on approach?
cards.answer: Wind shear is a sudden change in wind speed and/or direction over a short distance. On approach, you may notice unexpected airspeed fluctuations (±15 knots or more), unusual pitch attitude requirements to maintain glidepath, a sudden increase or decrease in sink rate, or difficulty maintaining a stabilized approach. Tower may also report wind shear alerts from LLWAS. If you encounter wind shear on approach, execute an immediate go-around: apply full power, pitch for Vy, and retract flaps incrementally. Do not attempt to salvage a destabilized approach.

cards.question: How does structural icing form and what should a VFR pilot do about it?
cards.answer: Structural icing forms when an aircraft flies through visible moisture (clouds, rain) at temperatures at or below freezing. Clear ice forms from large supercooled droplets hitting the airframe and flowing back before freezing—it's heavy and hard to remove. Rime ice forms from small droplets freezing instantly on contact—it's rough and milky. Mixed ice combines both. VFR pilots should avoid icing conditions entirely, as most GA aircraft are not certified for flight into known icing. If you inadvertently encounter icing, change altitude (usually climb above the clouds or descend to warmer air), notify ATC, and consider declaring an emergency.

cards.question: What is the significance of a Convective Outlook and how do you use it?
cards.answer: The Convective Outlook, issued by the Storm Prediction Center, categorizes thunderstorm risk for the next 1-3 days as TSTM (general), MRGL (marginal), SLGT (slight), ENH (enhanced), MDT (moderate), and HIGH. It helps with advance flight planning by highlighting areas where significant thunderstorm activity is expected. Even a MRGL risk means isolated severe storms are possible. For flight planning, I use the convective outlook to decide if a cross-country is feasible on a given day, identify alternative routing, and determine departure windows to avoid the peak convective heating period (typically 2-6 PM local).

cards.question: You check the weather and see mountain obscuration in the AIRMET Sierra. What does this mean for your flight?
cards.answer: An AIRMET Sierra for mountain obscuration means clouds or precipitation are obscuring mountains and ridgelines, making terrain identification and clearance extremely hazardous for VFR flight. This is one of the deadliest weather situations in GA—controlled flight into terrain. If your route crosses mountainous terrain covered by this AIRMET, you should either cancel the flight, route around the affected area, or wait for conditions to improve. Even if the valleys are clear, ridges and passes may be obscured. This is not a condition to 'try and see'—mountain obscuration demands a firm no-go decision.

cards.question: How do you obtain current radar imagery for your planned route, and what are its limitations?
cards.answer: Radar imagery is available through aviationweather.gov, ForeFlight, and other EFB apps. ADS-B In (via FIS-B) provides in-cockpit radar imagery. However, radar has important limitations: the data displayed on ADS-B and other sources can be 5-20 minutes old, which is an eternity for fast-moving cells. Radar shows precipitation intensity, not turbulence directly. It cannot detect clear air turbulence, icing, or wind shear. Ground-based radar has gaps in coverage at low altitudes and in mountainous terrain. Never use cockpit radar displays for threading between thunderstorm cells—they are for strategic avoidance only.

cards.question: You notice that the forecast calls for IFR conditions at your destination at your planned arrival time, but VFR conditions before and after. What do you do?
cards.answer: This is a go/no-go decision that requires careful analysis. First, understand why IFR conditions are expected—is it morning fog that will burn off? A frontal passage? Radiation fog? Second, assess your flexibility: can you delay departure to arrive during VFR conditions? Do you have an alternate airport with VFR conditions within fuel range? Third, consider your personal minimums and experience. As a VFR-only pilot, planning to arrive when IFR conditions are forecast is unacceptable. Either adjust your timing, select an alternate destination, or cancel. Never plan a VFR flight expecting to arrive in IFR conditions.

cards.question: What are the VFR fuel requirements for a day cross-country flight?
cards.answer: Per 14 CFR 91.151, for VFR day flight, you must have enough fuel to fly to the first point of intended landing and, assuming normal cruising speed, to fly after that for at least 30 minutes. For VFR night, the reserve increases to 45 minutes. These are minimums—good practice dictates carrying substantially more. When calculating fuel requirements, use the POH fuel burn figures for your planned altitude and power setting, add a margin for headwinds, and account for taxi, climb, and descent fuel. Never plan a flight where you arrive with only the minimum reserve.

cards.question: How and when do you file a VFR flight plan, and what happens if you don't close it?
cards.answer: You can file a VFR flight plan through 1800wxbrief.com, the Leidos Flight Service app, by calling Flight Service, or through some EFB apps. It should be filed before departure and activated by contacting Flight Service once airborne (or requesting activation on the ground). A VFR flight plan is not mandatory (except for certain international flights) but highly recommended. You must close it upon arrival by calling Flight Service—if you don't, after 30 minutes past your ETA, Flight Service will attempt to contact you and eventually initiate search and rescue. An unclosed flight plan wastes SAR resources and can result in a large bill.

cards.question: Walk me through how you would build a navigation log for a cross-country flight.
cards.answer: I start by drawing the course line on the sectional chart and identifying checkpoints every 10-20 miles. For each leg, I measure the true course, apply magnetic variation to get magnetic heading, then apply wind correction using the winds aloft forecast to get true heading, then magnetic heading, and finally compass heading (accounting for deviation). I calculate groundspeed for each leg using the wind triangle. I then compute time enroute for each leg, cumulative fuel burn, and identify radio frequencies needed. I note the appropriate VFR cruising altitude per the hemispheric rule, planned altitudes for terrain clearance, and emergency airports along the route.

cards.question: What is the hemispheric rule for VFR cruising altitudes?
cards.answer: Per 14 CFR 91.159, when flying more than 3,000 feet AGL on a magnetic course of 0° through 179°, fly at an odd thousand plus 500 feet (3,500, 5,500, 7,500, etc.). On a magnetic course of 180° through 359°, fly at an even thousand plus 500 feet (4,500, 6,500, 8,500, etc.). The +500 provides separation from IFR traffic flying at even/odd thousands. This rule applies above 3,000 AGL in uncontrolled airspace and is advisory in controlled airspace unless ATC assigns a specific altitude. Memory aid: 'East is odd, west is even' based on magnetic course.

cards.question: How do you check for NOTAMs, and what types should you be aware of?
cards.answer: NOTAMs are checked through 1800wxbrief.com, the Leidos Pilot Web portal, or EFB apps like ForeFlight. There are several types: D-NOTAMs cover airport/facility conditions (runway closures, NAVAID outages), FDC NOTAMs cover regulatory changes (instrument approach amendments, TFRs, airspace changes), and Military NOTAMs cover military facility information. TFRs (Temporary Flight Restrictions) are issued as FDC NOTAMs and are critical to check—violating a TFR can result in enforcement action, interception, or worse. Always check NOTAMs as close to departure time as possible, as they can be issued at any time.

cards.question: How do you check for Temporary Flight Restrictions along your route?
cards.answer: TFRs are checked through the FAA's TFR website (tfr.faa.gov), 1800wxbrief.com, ForeFlight, or by requesting a briefing from Flight Service. Always check graphically—some TFRs are complex shapes. Common TFR types include presidential/VIP movement (P-40 and P-56 style), sporting events (stadiums with 30,000+ capacity, 3 NM radius, surface to 3,000 AGL), disaster/hazard areas, and military operations. TFRs can be issued with very short notice. Violating a TFR can result in certificate action, civil penalties, and potentially being intercepted by military aircraft.

cards.question: You're planning a cross-country and your calculated fuel burn will leave you with exactly 30 minutes reserve during the day. Is this acceptable?
cards.answer: While it technically meets the legal minimum under 14 CFR 91.151 for VFR day, this is poor planning and I would not launch with this plan. The regulation establishes the bare minimum, not a safe practice. Your fuel burn calculations assume normal conditions—unexpected headwinds, routing around weather, a diversion to an alternate, or higher-than-planned fuel flow could eliminate that margin. I would plan for at least one hour of reserve beyond my planned destination, ideally more. If the numbers are tight, consider a fuel stop enroute—it adds a few minutes but removes significant risk.

cards.question: How do you convert between true course, magnetic heading, and compass heading?
cards.answer: Start with the true course measured from the sectional chart. Add or subtract magnetic variation (found on isogonic lines) to get magnetic course—'East is least, West is best' meaning subtract easterly variation. Then apply wind correction angle to get magnetic heading. Finally, apply compass deviation from the deviation card in the aircraft to get compass heading. For example: true course 090°, 10°E variation → magnetic course 080°, 5° right wind correction → magnetic heading 085°, deviation card shows +2° on east → compass heading 087°.

cards.question: What factors do you consider when selecting checkpoints for a cross-country flight?
cards.answer: Good checkpoints are easily identifiable from the air, unique in the area, and spaced 10-20 miles apart. Ideal features include: intersections of highways and railroads, distinctive lakes or rivers, towns with visible landmarks, airports, and prominent terrain features. Avoid using features that are hard to see (small ponds, individual buildings) or too common in the area (if there are many lakes, one lake looks like another). Choose checkpoints that are near your course line, not miles away. Having checkpoints at consistent intervals allows you to track your groundspeed and ETA accuracy.

cards.question: When is a transponder required for VFR flight?
cards.answer: A transponder with Mode C altitude reporting is required: (1) in or above Class B airspace; (2) in Class C airspace; (3) in the Mode C veil (generally within 30 NM of the primary Class B airport, from the surface to 10,000 MSL); (4) above 10,000 feet MSL (except at or below 2,500 AGL); and (5) within the contiguous US above the ceiling of Class B or C up to 10,000 MSL. Per 14 CFR 91.215, you can request a deviation from ATC to operate without a transponder in some of these areas, but it must be approved before entering.

cards.question: Show me how you would plan a diversion to an alternate airport during a cross-country.
cards.answer: If I need to divert in flight, I first identify the nearest suitable alternate airport using my sectional chart or EFB. I estimate the heading to fly using the chart (finger method or protractor). I note the distance and estimate the time enroute based on my current groundspeed. I calculate if I have sufficient fuel to reach the alternate with adequate reserves. I tune to the alternate airport's CTAF or approach frequency, obtain weather if possible (ATIS/ASOS), and notify ATC or Flight Service if in contact. I also reconsider whether the situation warrants declaring an emergency for priority handling.

cards.question: How do you calculate the wind correction angle and groundspeed for your flight?
cards.answer: You can use a manual E6B flight computer, an electronic E6B, or a flight planning app. On the manual E6B, set the wind direction under the true index, mark the wind speed up from the center grommet, then rotate to place your true course under the index. The dot's displacement from centerline gives your wind correction angle (left or right of course), and its position relative to the groundspeed arc gives your groundspeed. In practice, many pilots use electronic tools, but understanding the manual process demonstrates knowledge of the wind triangle relationship between true airspeed, wind, groundspeed, and course.

cards.question: You filed a VFR flight plan and your ETE was 2 hours. You're now 30 minutes behind schedule due to headwinds. What should you do?
cards.answer: You should contact Flight Service (122.0 or via radio) to amend your ETA. If you don't update and arrive more than 30 minutes past your filed ETA without closing the flight plan, Search and Rescue procedures will be initiated. This wastes valuable SAR resources and can cause significant concern. You can update your ETA, route, altitude, or any other flight plan information in the air. This is also a good time to recalculate your fuel situation—if headwinds are stronger than forecast, you may arrive with less fuel than planned and should consider a fuel stop.

cards.question: What are the key symbols you should be able to identify on a sectional chart?
cards.answer: Critical sectional chart symbols include: airport symbols (blue for towered, magenta for non-towered), airspace boundaries and altitudes, obstructions (with heights MSL and AGL), terrain elevation contours, VORs, special use airspace (MOAs, restricted, prohibited), TFR areas, isogonic lines for magnetic variation, maximum elevation figures in each lat/long quadrangle, and communication frequencies in airport data blocks. You should also recognize floor altitudes for Class E airspace (the faded magenta and blue lines), Victor airways, and the Mode C veil around Class B airports.

cards.question: What is the purpose of maximum elevation figures on a sectional chart?
cards.answer: Maximum Elevation Figures (MEFs) are printed in large numbers in each latitude/longitude quadrangle on the sectional chart. They represent the highest known feature (terrain or obstruction) within that quadrangle, rounded up to the next 100-foot value, plus a safety margin. They provide a quick reference for terrain and obstruction clearance. If you're flying at or above the MEF for your quadrangle, you are guaranteed to clear all charted obstacles. However, MEFs don't account for uncharted obstacles, so maintaining additional altitude margin is prudent.

cards.question: What is the difference between true airspeed, indicated airspeed, calibrated airspeed, and groundspeed?
cards.answer: Indicated Airspeed (IAS) is what you read on the airspeed indicator. Calibrated Airspeed (CAS) corrects IAS for instrument and position errors (found in the POH). True Airspeed (TAS) corrects CAS for non-standard pressure and temperature at altitude—it's the actual speed through the air mass and increases about 2% per 1,000 feet. Groundspeed (GS) is your actual speed over the ground, which is TAS adjusted for wind. For flight planning, you need TAS for the wind triangle and GS for computing enroute time and fuel burn.

cards.question: You discover your destination airport has a NOTAM for runway construction. How does this affect your planning?
cards.answer: A runway construction NOTAM could close or shorten the runway, change available taxi routes, or create temporary obstacles. I would determine which runway is closed and whether the remaining runways can accommodate my aircraft considering wind conditions. I'd check for displaced thresholds, reduced runway length, and any temporary taxiway restrictions. If the available runway is significantly shorter, I need to recalculate landing distance considering density altitude and wind. I might need to select an alternate airport if the remaining runway doesn't provide adequate safety margin for my aircraft.

cards.question: How far in advance should you check weather and NOTAMs for a cross-country flight?
cards.answer: Start monitoring weather several days out using the convective outlook and extended forecasts to pick the best day. Get a detailed outlook briefing 12-24 hours before the planned flight. Obtain a standard briefing the morning of the flight. Then do a final update within an hour of departure for any last-minute changes—especially TFRs and significant weather updates. NOTAMs should be checked within a few hours of departure since they can be issued at any time. This layered approach gives you the best picture and the most time to make good decisions rather than feeling rushed.

cards.question: What information is included in an airport's Chart Supplement (formerly A/FD) entry?
cards.answer: The Chart Supplement entry includes: airport location and geographic coordinates, field elevation, runway data (length, width, surface, lighting), traffic pattern altitude and direction, communication frequencies (CTAF, tower, ground, ATIS/ASOS/AWOS), NAVAIDs, services available (fuel, parking, maintenance), attendance hours, remarks (noise abatement, special procedures, hazards), and airport diagram reference. It's a critical planning document—I use it to determine fuel availability, confirm runway lengths for performance planning, get communication frequencies, and learn about any special procedures or hazards at the airport.

cards.question: You need to fly over a sparsely populated area where there are no suitable emergency landing sites for 30 miles. How does this affect your planning?
cards.answer: This adds risk that must be mitigated. First, I would ensure the aircraft is in excellent mechanical condition. I would fly at a higher altitude to give more glide range and options in an engine-out scenario—every 1,000 feet of altitude gives roughly 1.5-2 NM of glide range in most trainers. I would carry survival equipment appropriate for the terrain (water, first aid, signaling devices). I would file a flight plan so SAR would be activated if I went missing. I would ensure my ELT is functional and consider carrying a PLB (Personal Locator Beacon) for faster rescue response. I would also brief my passengers on emergency procedures.

cards.question: You're planning to fly through Class B airspace. What do you need?
cards.answer: To enter Class B airspace, you need: (1) a specific ATC clearance—you must hear the words 'cleared into the Class Bravo'; (2) a two-way radio; (3) a transponder with Mode C altitude reporting; (4) at least a private pilot certificate (student pilots need a specific endorsement for that Class B). Weather minimums are 3 SM visibility and clear of clouds. Speed is limited to 200 knots below 2,500 feet AGL within 4 NM of the primary airport. Note that 'radar contact' or 'squawk xxxx' is NOT a clearance to enter.

cards.question: What are the requirements to fly through Class C airspace?
cards.answer: Class C requires: (1) two-way radio communication established with ATC before entry—you must receive a response that includes your callsign; (2) a transponder with Mode C. VFR weather minimums are 3 SM visibility, 500 below, 1,000 above, 2,000 horizontal from clouds. Class C typically extends from the surface to 4,000 feet above the airport elevation with an inner circle (5 NM radius) and outer circle (10 NM radius). There is also a 20 NM outer area where participation is voluntary but recommended. Unlike Class B, you do not need an explicit clearance—establishing communication is sufficient.

cards.question: What constitutes 'establishing two-way radio communication' with Class C?
cards.answer: Communication is established when ATC responds to your initial call using your aircraft callsign. If the controller responds with your full callsign and says 'standby,' communication is established and you may enter. However, if they respond with 'aircraft calling, standby'—without your callsign—communication is NOT established and you may NOT enter. The key distinction is whether ATC has acknowledged YOUR specific aircraft. Always listen for your callsign in the response before entering the airspace.

cards.question: Describe Class D airspace and its requirements.
cards.answer: Class D airspace typically surrounds airports with an operating control tower. It extends from the surface to 2,500 feet AGL (shown on charts with a dashed blue line and altitude in hundreds of feet MSL). Requirements: establish two-way radio communication before entry (same standard as Class C). VFR weather minimums are 3 SM visibility, 500 below, 1,000 above, 2,000 horizontal from clouds. When the tower closes, Class D typically reverts to Class E or G (check the Chart Supplement). No transponder is required by the Class D airspace itself, but you may need one if within a Mode C veil.

cards.question: What are the different configurations of Class E airspace?
cards.answer: Class E is controlled airspace that isn't A, B, C, or D. It exists in several forms: (1) surface-based Class E (dashed magenta line) around airports with instrument approaches but no tower; (2) Class E beginning at 700 feet AGL (magenta fade/vignette) to protect instrument approaches; (3) Class E beginning at 1,200 feet AGL (blue fade/vignette or absence of magenta fade); (4) Federal airways (8 NM wide, from 1,200 AGL to 17,999 MSL); and (5) Class E above 14,500 MSL in the contiguous US. Understanding Class E matters because VFR weather minimums differ depending on whether you're in Class E or Class G.

cards.question: What is Class G airspace and what VFR weather minimums apply?
cards.answer: Class G is uncontrolled airspace where ATC has no authority or responsibility to provide separation. It exists primarily below the floor of Class E airspace. VFR weather minimums vary by altitude and time of day. Below 1,200 AGL during the day: 1 SM visibility, clear of clouds. Below 1,200 AGL at night: 3 SM visibility, 500 below, 1,000 above, 2,000 horizontal. Above 1,200 AGL but below 10,000 MSL: 1 SM visibility, 500 below, 1,000 above, 2,000 horizontal during day; 3 SM visibility at night with the same cloud clearances. Above 10,000 MSL: 5 SM visibility, 1,000 below, 1,000 above, 1 SM horizontal.

cards.question: You see a blue segmented circle with a number on the sectional chart around an airport. What does it mean?
cards.answer: A blue dashed line circle around an airport indicates Class D airspace. The number inside (in hundreds of feet MSL, shown with a minus sign like [25] meaning up to but not including 2,500 MSL) indicates the ceiling of the airspace. If you see [T] next to the altitude, it means part-time—check Chart Supplement for tower hours. When the tower is closed, the Class D becomes Class E or G. Remember that blue represents towered airports on sectional charts, while magenta represents non-towered airports.

cards.question: What is the Mode C veil and what does it require?
cards.answer: The Mode C veil is a 30 NM ring (generally) around the primary airport of Class B airspace, depicted on the sectional chart. Within this veil, from the surface to 10,000 feet MSL, all aircraft must have an operating transponder with Mode C (altitude reporting) capability, regardless of whether they intend to enter the Class B airspace. This allows ATC and TCAS to see traffic in the busy terminal area. You can request a deviation from the Mode C requirement from ATC if your transponder is inoperative, but this must be arranged before entering the veil.

cards.question: What are Special Use Airspace categories and how do they affect VFR flight?
cards.answer: Prohibited Areas (P-xxx) are completely off-limits to all aircraft—no exceptions without the using agency's permission. Restricted Areas (R-xxx) contain hazards like artillery firing or missile testing; you cannot enter when 'hot' without ATC clearance or permission from the controlling agency. MOAs (Military Operations Areas) have military training activity; VFR traffic is not prohibited but exercise extreme caution, and ATC can provide information on MOA activity. Warning Areas extend offshore and contain similar hazards to restricted areas. Alert Areas have high pilot training or unusual aerial activity—again, not prohibited but use caution.

cards.question: What is Special VFR and when would you request it?
cards.answer: Special VFR allows you to operate within the lateral boundaries of surface-based controlled airspace (Class B, C, D, or surface E) with lower weather minimums: 1 SM visibility and clear of clouds (instead of 3 SM and standard cloud clearances). You must request it from ATC—they cannot offer it. At night, you must be instrument rated and in an IFR-capable aircraft. Some airports (listed in 14 CFR 91 Appendix D) prohibit Special VFR operations due to traffic density. It's a useful tool for departing an airport when visibility is marginal but conditions outside the controlled airspace are VFR.

cards.question: What speed restrictions apply in different airspace?
cards.answer: Below 10,000 feet MSL: maximum 250 knots indicated (14 CFR 91.117). Within 4 NM of the primary airport of Class B or within or beneath Class B: 200 knots indicated. In a Class C or D surface area: 200 knots indicated. In VFR corridors through Class B: 200 knots. Beneath Class B airspace: 200 knots. Above 10,000 MSL: no speed restriction for VFR. These restrictions exist for collision avoidance—closing rates increase with speed, and the 'see and avoid' concept requires adequate reaction time.

cards.question: You're flying VFR at 8,500 feet MSL outside of controlled airspace. What weather minimums apply?
cards.answer: At 8,500 MSL, you could be in Class E or Class G airspace, depending on the area. In Class E (above 1,200 AGL, below 10,000 MSL): 3 SM visibility, 500 below, 1,000 above, 2,000 horizontal from clouds. In Class G (above 1,200 AGL, below 10,000 MSL during the day): 1 SM visibility, 500 below, 1,000 above, 2,000 horizontal from clouds. The difference is significant—Class G allows only 1 SM visibility during the day while Class E requires 3 SM. You need to verify which airspace you're in by checking the sectional chart.

cards.question: What are VFR weather minimums at or above 10,000 feet MSL?
cards.answer: At or above 10,000 feet MSL (and more than 1,200 feet AGL), VFR weather minimums are the same regardless of airspace class (E or G): 5 SM visibility, 1,000 feet below clouds, 1,000 feet above clouds, and 1 SM horizontal from clouds. The increased minimums are necessary because aircraft at higher altitudes fly faster (higher TAS for the same IAS), so more visibility and cloud clearance is needed for see-and-avoid to work effectively. These minimums also provide better protection against encountering IFR traffic departing clouds.

cards.question: What is a TRSA and what services are provided?
cards.answer: A Terminal Radar Service Area (TRSA) is depicted on sectional charts with a solid dark gray line. TRSAs are not regulatory airspace—participation is voluntary for VFR pilots but recommended. When you contact approach control within a TRSA, you receive radar sequencing and separation services similar to Class C operations. There is no specific equipment requirement beyond a two-way radio. TRSAs were the predecessor to Class C airspace—some airports were converted to Class C while others remained TRSAs. If you choose not to participate, you can still transit the area, but you lose the safety benefits of radar services.

cards.question: You are flying VFR and hear 'remain clear of Class Bravo airspace.' Have you been cleared into Class B?
cards.answer: Absolutely not. 'Remain clear of Class Bravo airspace' is an explicit instruction to stay OUT. You need to hear the specific words 'cleared into the Class Bravo airspace' or 'cleared through the Class Bravo airspace' to legally enter. Controllers may provide traffic advisories, altitude assignments, and vectors while you remain outside—but none of these constitute a clearance to enter. If you need to enter, specifically request it: 'Request clearance through the Class Bravo.' Don't assume a squawk code, radar vectors, or flight following is a Bravo clearance.

cards.question: What are right-of-way rules in the air?
cards.answer: Per 14 CFR 91.113: an aircraft in distress has right-of-way over all other aircraft. When two aircraft are converging at approximately the same altitude, the aircraft to the other's right has right-of-way. An aircraft being overtaken has right-of-way—the overtaking aircraft must pass to the right. When two aircraft are approaching head-on, each shall alter course to the right. Aircraft on final approach or landing have right-of-way over other aircraft in flight or on the surface, except that this doesn't allow forcing another aircraft off the runway. Specific precedence: balloon, then glider, then airship, then airplane/helicopter.

cards.question: How do you identify the boundaries of Class E airspace on a sectional chart?
cards.answer: Surface-based Class E has a dashed magenta line around the airport boundary. Class E starting at 700 feet AGL is shown by a magenta fade (vignette) that fades from magenta outward. Class E starting at 1,200 feet AGL is shown by a blue fade, or it's the default when no other shading exists. Federal airways (Class E from 1,200 AGL to 17,999 MSL) are shown as light blue lines. Where there is no shading or symbology, Class E begins at 14,500 MSL. The key skill is reading these subtle shading differences to determine exact airspace boundaries and floors at your planned altitude.

cards.question: You want to fly through a Restricted Area. How can you determine if it's active?
cards.answer: First, check the sectional chart and Chart Supplement for the using agency, hours of operation, and altitudes. Many restricted areas are only active during specific hours or days and are depicted with 'times of use' information. Contact the controlling ARTCC or the using agency directly to determine current status. ATC can often advise whether a restricted area is 'hot' (active) or 'cold' (inactive). If cold, ATC may authorize you through. If hot, you must route around it. Never assume a restricted area is inactive—verify before entering. The consequences of entering an active restricted area include danger from military activity and enforcement action.

cards.question: What are VFR corridors and VFR transition routes through Class B airspace?
cards.answer: VFR corridors are defined passages through Class B airspace that do not require an ATC clearance. They are depicted on Terminal Area Charts (TACs). VFR transition routes are specific routes through Class B airspace that DO require ATC clearance but provide a defined path. The difference is important: corridors are 'holes' in Class B where you can fly without talking to anyone (though it's recommended), while transition routes require contact with ATC and a clearance. Both are designed to allow VFR traffic to transit busy terminal areas safely. Check the TAC for specific corridor dimensions, altitudes, and reporting points.

cards.question: A MOA is shown along your route. Should you be concerned?
cards.answer: Yes, exercise caution. While VFR flight through a MOA is permitted without ATC clearance, military training activity there may include high-speed, high-performance aircraft practicing aerobatics, intercepts, and formation flying. Contact the controlling ARTCC or FSS to check if the MOA is active and what type of activity is occurring. If active, consider routing around it or, if you must transit, obtain flight following so ATC can provide traffic information. Fly with your transponder on and lights on. Be extra vigilant for high-speed traffic that can close on you faster than you can react.

cards.question: Show me how you calculated the weight and balance for today's flight.
cards.answer: I start by looking up the aircraft's basic empty weight and moment from the POH or most recent weight and balance sheet. Then I add each item: pilot/front passenger weight × arm, rear passengers × arm, baggage × arm, and fuel weight (6 lbs/gallon for avgas) × arm. I sum the total weight and total moment, then divide moment by weight to find the CG position. I verify the total weight is within the maximum gross weight and the CG falls within the approved envelope shown in the POH. I also check that the CG remains within limits as fuel burns off during flight, since the CG can shift.

cards.question: What happens if the CG is too far forward?
cards.answer: A forward CG increases stability but requires more back pressure (or nose-up trim) to maintain level flight, which increases drag and fuel burn. The most critical concern is during landing—you may not have enough elevator authority to flare properly, leading to a hard, nose-first landing that can cause a prop strike or nose gear failure. Forward CG also increases stall speed because the tail must produce more downward force, effectively increasing the wing loading. Always verify CG is within limits; just because you're under max gross weight doesn't mean the CG is acceptable.

cards.question: What happens if the CG is too far aft?
cards.answer: An aft CG decreases longitudinal stability, making the aircraft pitch-sensitive and difficult to control. In extreme cases, the aircraft can become uncontrollable—a slight pitch input can result in an unrecoverable pitch-up. Stall recovery becomes more difficult or impossible because the elevator may not produce enough nose-down force. Aft CG also lowers the stall speed, which may sound good but means the aircraft is closer to an unrecoverable stall/spin condition. An aft CG beyond limits is one of the most dangerous conditions in aviation and is a common factor in fatal accidents.

cards.question: It's 95°F at an airport elevation of 5,000 feet. How does this affect your aircraft performance?
cards.answer: This combination creates very high density altitude—approximately 8,500 feet or more. The thin, hot air significantly degrades performance in every phase of flight: takeoff ground roll increases dramatically (possibly doubling or tripling), rate of climb decreases substantially (you may not be able to clear obstacles), engine power output decreases because less air enters the intake, and propeller efficiency drops. You must calculate performance using the POH charts for these conditions. It may be wise to reduce weight, plan for a longer runway, depart in the early morning when temperatures are lower, or not fly at all if performance margins are too thin.

cards.question: How do you calculate density altitude?
cards.answer: Density altitude can be calculated by first finding pressure altitude (set the altimeter to 29.92 and read the altitude, or use the formula: field elevation + (29.92 − current altimeter setting) × 1,000). Then correct for temperature: for every 1°C above standard temperature at that pressure altitude, add about 120 feet. Standard temperature at sea level is 15°C and decreases 2°C per 1,000 feet. You can also use the density altitude chart in the POH, the E6B, or a density altitude calculator. Many ASOS/AWOS stations broadcast density altitude when it exceeds field elevation by a significant amount.

cards.question: What is maneuvering speed (Va) and why does it change with weight?
cards.answer: Va is the maximum speed at which you can make one full, abrupt control input without exceeding the aircraft's structural load limits. Above Va, an abrupt full deflection could overstress the structure. Va decreases at lighter weights because at a lighter weight, the aircraft can generate more G-forces before stalling—and the stall is what protects the structure. At max gross weight, the aircraft stalls before reaching the structural limit, but at lighter weight it can reach higher G-forces before stalling. Most POHs list Va at max gross weight; you must calculate a lower Va for lighter weights. Importantly, Va does not protect against multiple successive control inputs or continuous turbulence.

cards.question: What are the V-speeds you should know and what do they mean?
cards.answer: Key V-speeds include: Vs0 (stall speed in landing configuration—bottom of the white arc), Vs1 (stall speed clean—bottom of the green arc), Vx (best angle of climb—most altitude in shortest distance), Vy (best rate of climb—most altitude in least time), Va (maneuvering speed), Vfe (maximum flap extended speed—top of white arc), Vno (maximum structural cruising speed—top of green arc, beginning of yellow arc), Vne (never exceed speed—red line). The yellow arc is the caution range—fly there only in smooth air. Each speed has a specific purpose for safe operation.

cards.question: How do you use the takeoff performance chart in the POH?
cards.answer: The takeoff performance chart accounts for pressure altitude, temperature (or density altitude), aircraft weight, wind component, and runway surface/condition. I enter with the current conditions: field elevation corrected to pressure altitude, the temperature, and my calculated takeoff weight. I read across for ground roll distance and total distance to clear a 50-foot obstacle. Then I apply corrections for headwind (reduces distance) or tailwind (increases distance substantially—generally 10% increase per 2 knots of tailwind). I also add a safety factor—the published numbers assume a new aircraft with a skilled pilot in ideal conditions. I typically add 50% for real-world margin.

cards.question: What load factor is imposed in a 60-degree bank turn?
cards.answer: In a level, coordinated 60-degree bank turn, the load factor is 2G. This means the wings must support twice the aircraft's weight. Load factor increases with bank angle: 30° = 1.15G, 45° = 1.41G, 60° = 2G, 75° = 3.86G. At 2G, the stall speed increases by approximately 41% (multiply by the square root of the load factor). This means if your normal stall speed is 50 knots, in a 60-degree bank it's about 70 knots. This is why steep turns require additional back pressure and airspeed awareness—the accelerated stall speed is much closer to your flying speed.

cards.question: How does weight affect aircraft performance?
cards.answer: Increased weight degrades performance in virtually every measurable way: higher takeoff and landing speeds (and distances), reduced rate and angle of climb, lower cruise speed at the same power setting (or more fuel burn to maintain speed), higher stall speed (proportional to the square root of the weight increase), reduced range and endurance, and higher approach speeds. The effect is compounded at high density altitudes. This is why weight and balance isn't just a regulatory exercise—it directly impacts your safety margins. Never exceed max gross weight, and when performance is marginal (hot/high conditions), reducing weight is one of the most effective mitigations.

cards.question: What are the structural load limits for a normal category airplane?
cards.answer: A normal category airplane is certified for load factors of +3.8G to -1.52G. This means the structure can handle 3.8 times the aircraft weight in positive loading and 1.52 times in negative loading. Utility category allows +4.4G to -1.76G and permits limited aerobatics (spins, lazy eights, steep turns, chandelles). Acrobatic category allows +6G to -3G. These limits apply to new aircraft—fatigue, corrosion, and age can reduce actual structural capability. Always operate within the certified category and avoid abrupt maneuvers or flight in severe turbulence that could approach these limits.

cards.question: How do you calculate the crosswind component for a landing?
cards.answer: Use the crosswind component chart in the POH or the rule of thumb method. If the wind is at a 30-degree angle to the runway, the crosswind component is about half the wind speed. At 45 degrees, it's about 70% of wind speed. At 60 degrees, it's about 87%. At 90 degrees (direct crosswind), it equals the full wind speed. For example, wind 270 at 20 knots on runway 24 (240°) = 30-degree angle, so about 10-knot crosswind. Compare this to the demonstrated crosswind component in the POH. Note that the 'demonstrated' value is not a limitation for Part 23 aircraft—it's the maximum tested during certification—but it serves as a practical guide for your personal limits.

cards.question: What is pressure altitude and how do you find it?
cards.answer: Pressure altitude is the altitude indicated when the altimeter is set to 29.92 inHg (standard pressure). It's used for performance calculations because aircraft performance depends on air density, not field elevation. To find it: set 29.92 in the Kollsman window and read the altimeter, or calculate: field elevation + ((29.92 − current altimeter setting) × 1,000). For example, at a field elevation of 3,000 feet with an altimeter setting of 29.72: 3,000 + ((29.92 − 29.72) × 1,000) = 3,000 + 200 = 3,200 feet pressure altitude.

cards.question: You're planning a flight with full fuel and four adults. The POH shows you're 30 pounds over gross weight. What are your options?
cards.answer: You cannot legally fly over gross weight. Options include: (1) reduce fuel—calculate the minimum fuel needed for the trip plus legal reserves (day VFR: 30 min, night: 45 min) and add a reasonable safety margin; (2) leave a passenger behind; (3) reduce baggage; (4) plan a fuel stop enroute so you depart with less fuel. Also recalculate the CG—removing weight from different locations affects the CG differently. Never rationalize flying overweight because 'it's just a little over'—the performance charts, structural limits, and certification basis all assume you're at or below max gross weight. Being overweight on a hot day at a short runway has killed many pilots.

cards.question: How does humidity affect aircraft performance?
cards.answer: High humidity reduces air density because water vapor is lighter than the nitrogen and oxygen it displaces. This increases the effective density altitude—up to 800 feet or more in extreme tropical conditions. The result is reduced engine power (less dense air for combustion), reduced propeller efficiency, and reduced wing lift. Unfortunately, standard density altitude calculations and most POH performance charts do not account for humidity. This means on a hot, humid day, actual performance will be slightly worse than what the charts predict. It's another reason to add a safety margin to your performance calculations.

cards.question: What is the relationship between flaps and aircraft performance?
cards.answer: Flaps increase lift coefficient (allowing slower flight) and also increase drag. The net effect depends on flap setting: first increments of flaps (10-20°) produce significant lift increase with moderate drag increase—useful for slow-flight and approach. Higher settings (30-40°) produce substantial drag increases, steepening the descent angle for short-field landings. Flaps allow a slower approach speed (lower over-the-fence speed), which reduces landing distance. However, flaps increase takeoff distance if used beyond the recommended setting because the drag penalty outweighs the lift benefit. Always use the POH-recommended flap settings for each type of operation.

cards.question: Why should you never exceed Vne?
cards.answer: Vne (never exceed speed, the red line on the airspeed indicator) is the maximum speed at which the aircraft has been shown to meet structural and flutter requirements.
